title: Implement /api/auth/login and /api/auth/register Next.js route handlers
description: Create Next.js route handler files at src/app/api/auth/login/route.ts and src/app/api/auth/register/route.ts that expose POST endpoints for JWT authentication. The route handlers should parse JSON request bodies, validate input using the existing mini-Zod schema (src/utils/schema.ts), delegate to the existing business logic (src/api/auth/login.ts and src/api/auth/register.ts), and return proper JSON responses with correct HTTP status codes (200 for success, 401 for invalid credentials, 409 for duplicate email, 400 for validation errors). Existing tests in src/api/auth/ already cover the business logic; route handlers need integration-level tests.
existing_patterns: src/utils/schema.ts — custom mini-Zod schema builder with string/number/boolean/array/object types, used for input validation

src/api/auth/login.ts — existing login business logic (exports login() async function accepting email, password, ipAddress, userAgent, userStore, sessionStore, jwtService, returns LoginResult with accessToken/refreshToken/user)
src/api/auth/register.ts — existing register business logic (exports register() async function accepting email, password, confirmPassword, ipAddress, userAgent, payload, authService)
src/app/api/enroll/route.ts — pattern for Next.js route handler with GET+withAuth, JSON request parsing, and Response with JSON serialization
src/app/api/health/route.ts — pattern for simple public POST route handler returning JSON Response
src/services/progress.ts — getPayloadInstance() async function used to get Payload singleton in route handlers
src/auth/auth-service.ts — AuthService.login() uses PBKDF2 password verification and returns AuthResult with accessToken/refreshToken/user; returns 401 for invalid credentials, no information leakage

questions.question: Should confirmPassword remain optional in the public API? The existing register.ts business logic validates it, but the acceptance criteria only specify { email, password } for the POST body. Should confirmPassword be accepted but optional, or should the API accept only { email, password }?

options.label: Keep optional (recommended)
options.description: API accepts { email, password } and optionally { confirmPassword }. When absent, skip confirmPassword validation in the handler. This matches the AC while preserving the existing business logic flow.

options.label: Remove from public API
options.description: API only accepts { email, password }. Modify the route handler to call the register business logic without confirmPassword (pass undefined or a dummy value).

questions.question: Should these public endpoints be protected with rate-limiting middleware (e.g., the existing rate-limiter) to prevent brute-force attacks, or should they remain unprotected?

options.label: No rate limiting (recommended)
options.description: Keep endpoints unprotected for simplicity. Auth brute-force protection can be added separately as a future enhancement.

options.label: Apply rate limiting
options.description: Apply existing rate-limiter middleware to both endpoints. Requires identifying and wiring in the existing rate-limiter from src/middleware/.

questions.question: Should Zod (the npm package) be added as a dependency for this feature, or should we continue using the custom mini-Zod at src/utils/schema.ts? The acceptance criteria specify 'Zod validation' but the project doesn't have zod in package.json.

options.label: Use mini-Zod (recommended)
options.description: Use the existing custom mini-Zod in src/utils/schema.ts. It supports string/number/boolean/array/object schemas with parse() and optional(). Adding zod would be unnecessary duplication.

options.label: Add zod as dependency
options.description: Add zod to package.json and use it instead of the custom mini-Zod. This adds a new dependency to the project.
